title: How Do You Make a Store Feel Premium? — Daniel Fox
meta_description: How do you make a store feel premium? Every answer comes back to something you can see — lighting, fixtures, materials, staff. But there's a premium…

schema.headline: How Do You Make a Store Feel Premium?
schema.description: How do you make a store feel premium? Every answer comes back to something you can see — lighting, fixtures, materials, staff.
about: How To Make A Store Feel Premium

faq.q: Can music really make a store feel more premium?
faq.a: It can nudge perceived value, yes — but it's a smaller, more conditional effect than the "play classical and look expensive" story implies. In Areni & Kim's 1993 wine-shop study, classical music (vs top-40) shifted shoppers toward pricier bottles — same stock, same prices. They didn't buy more bottles; they bought up the shelf. That's real, but it rests on a small set of single-store studies, which is why I grade it a B−.

faq.q: So should I just play classical music to seem upscale?
faq.a: No — and this is the part most people miss. Classical didn't work because it's universally "fancy." It worked because it fit what shoppers already believed about wine. North et al. (1999) showed the same thing in reverse: French music sold French wine, German music sold German wine. The lever is congruence — the music has to fit what you sell and who's in the room. Play classical in a skate shop and you've just made it confusing.

faq.q: What should I actually do with this?
faq.a: Put the music on the same list as the lighting and the fixtures — something the brand chooses on purpose, for fit, instead of a head-office playlist nobody picked. It's the cheapest premium signal you have and the one thing a customer can't get through a screen. If you want to know what your stores are actually saying right now, that's the work I do: danielchristopherfox.com Evidence grade for this one: B−. Full citations in the description.
